title: The loop that visits you most
subtitle: A journal prompt set for examining the recurring thought that runs at night.
excerpt: A journal prompt set from Day 14 of the SLEEP program. The prompts ask for a close examination of the specific thought loop that most commonly visits at night, not thoughts in general, but the one particular track that runs on repeat.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 14 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795), "Unwind Mental Loops."

content.text: A journal prompt set from Day 14 of the SLEEP program. The prompts examine one specific recurring thought rather than the general problem of overthinking. What is the exact thought? What does it want? What would happen if you stopped following it? Twenty minutes, no editing.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What is the thought that visits most often at night?
Write it out as specifically as possible. Not "I worry about money" but the actual thought, in the actual words it uses.
What does repeating this thought seem to be trying to accomplish?
Resolve something? Prevent something? Stay prepared? Write what the loop seems to believe repetition will achieve.
Has following this loop ever actually resolved the thing it's worried about?
Write the answer plainly. Has the nighttime version of this thought ever produced a solution, or does it only produce more of itself?
What would I need to trust in order to put this thought down at night?
Not forever, just until morning. What would have to be true about you, about the situation, for you to let it wait?
What do I say to myself when I'm about to engage with the loop at 3am that I could say instead?
Write one sentence you could use in that moment. Something true and short enough to say half-asleep.
Adapted from Day 14 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
